title: CronJobs & Maintenance Tasks
description: How SAP Commerce Cloud schedules and executes background tasks using the CronJob framework, triggers, and job performers.

sections.title: The CronJob Framework — Core Concepts
sections.content:
The CronJob framework is SAP Commerce Cloud's built-in mechanism for scheduling and executing background tasks. It is used for everything from Solr indexing and order processing to data cleanup and report generation.

The framework has three distinct concepts that are often confused:

  • Job (ServicelayerJob) — the Spring bean that contains the actual business logic to execute. Defined once, reusable across multiple CronJob instances.
  • CronJob — a persisted item in the database that references a Job and holds configuration (parameters, logging settings, node group). Think of it as a scheduled run configuration.
  • Trigger — a persisted item that defines when a CronJob should run, using either a cron expression or a time interval.

This separation is intentional. You write one Job implementation, then create multiple CronJob instances that point to it — each with different parameters or schedules. For example, one SolrIndexerJob can power both a full index CronJob (nightly) and a partial update CronJob (every 15 minutes).

Key mental model: Job = what to do. CronJob = the configuration of a run. Trigger = when to run it. All three are required for a scheduled background task. The exam regularly tests whether candidates understand this three-part structure.

sections.title: Implementing a Job — ServicelayerJob
sections.content:
All modern CronJob implementations extend AbstractJobPerformable, which is the ServiceLayer-compatible base class. The older JobPerformable interface is still valid but AbstractJobPerformable provides useful utilities and is the recommended approach.

public class SendNewsletterJob
        extends AbstractJobPerformable<CronJobModel> {

    private NewsletterService newsletterService;
    private UserService userService;

    @Override
    public PerformResult perform(CronJobModel cronJob) {

        // Set admin user context — required for most service calls
        userService.setCurrentUser(
            userService.getAdminUser());

        try {
            newsletterService.sendPendingNewsletters();
            return new PerformResult(
                CronJobResult.SUCCESS,
                CronJobStatus.FINISHED);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            LOG.error("Newsletter job failed", e);
            return new PerformResult(
                CronJobResult.ERROR,
                CronJobStatus.FINISHED);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public boolean isAbortable() {
        return true; // Allow manual abort from HAC
    }

    // Setters for Spring injection
    public void setNewsletterService(
            NewsletterService newsletterService) {
        this.newsletterService = newsletterService;
    }
}

PerformResult

The perform() method must return a PerformResult combining a result and a status:

  • CronJobResult: SUCCESS, FAILURE, ERROR, UNKNOWN
  • CronJobStatus: FINISHED, ABORTED, RUNNING, UNKNOWN

The typical success return is new PerformResult(CronJobResult.SUCCESS, CronJobStatus.FINISHED).

Always set the user context. CronJobs run as the anonymous user by default. Most ServiceLayer calls require an authenticated user. Set the current user to admin (or a dedicated service account) at the start of perform() and restore it in a finally block to avoid permission errors.

sections.title: Registering a Job and Creating a CronJob via ImpEx
sections.content:
Once the Job class is written and wired as a Spring bean, you need to persist a ServicelayerJobModel pointing to it, and then create a CronJobModel referencing that job.

Spring Bean Declaration

<bean id="sendNewsletterJob"
      class="com.mycompany.SendNewsletterJob">
  <property name="newsletterService"
            ref="newsletterService"/>
  <property name="userService"
            ref="userService"/>
</bean>

ImpEx to Create the Job and CronJob

-- Create the ServicelayerJob item pointing to the Spring bean
INSERT_UPDATE ServicelayerJob; springId[unique=true]    ; code[unique=true]
                              ; sendNewsletterJob       ; sendNewsletterJob

-- Create the CronJob referencing the job
INSERT_UPDATE CronJob; code[unique=true]      ; job(code)         ; active
                     ; sendNewsletterCronJob  ; sendNewsletterJob ; true

-- Create a Trigger with a cron expression (every day at 2am)
INSERT_UPDATE Trigger; cronJob(code)[unique=true] ; cronExpression
                     ; sendNewsletterCronJob      ; 0 0 2 * * ?

The springId on ServicelayerJob must exactly match the Spring bean ID. This is how the platform finds and instantiates the correct job implementation at runtime.

springId must match exactly: A mismatch between the springId in the database and the actual Spring bean ID causes the CronJob to fail at runtime with a bean-not-found error. This is one of the most common CronJob setup mistakes and appears regularly in exam scenarios.

sections.title: Triggers and Cron Expressions
sections.content:
A Trigger defines when a CronJob runs. Commerce Cloud supports two trigger types:

  • Cron expression triggers — schedule based on a standard cron syntax
  • Relative triggers — run a fixed interval after the previous execution completes

Cron Expression Format

Commerce Cloud uses the Quartz cron expression format, which has 6 fields (not 5 like Unix cron):

┌─────────── second (0-59)
│ ┌───────── minute (0-59)
│ │ ┌─────── hour (0-23)
│ │ │ ┌───── day of month (1-31)
│ │ │ │ ┌─── month (1-12 or JAN-DEC)
│ │ │ │ │ ┌─ day of week (1-7 or SUN-SAT)
│ │ │ │ │ │
0 0 2 * * ?   ← Every day at 02:00:00
0 0/15 * * * ? ← Every 15 minutes
0 0 0 ? * MON ← Every Monday at midnight

The ? character means "no specific value" and is used in either the day-of-month or day-of-week field (not both).

Multiple Triggers per CronJob

A single CronJob can have multiple Triggers. This is useful when you need a task to run on different schedules — for example, a full run on Sundays and a lighter run every night.

Activating and Deactivating

Triggers have an active flag. Setting active=false on a Trigger disables it without deleting it. The CronJob itself also has an active flag — if the CronJob is inactive, none of its triggers fire.

Quartz vs Unix cron: The 6-field Quartz format is a common exam trap. Unix cron has 5 fields; Quartz has 6 (leading seconds field). An expression like 0 0 2 * * ? is valid Quartz. Writing 0 2 * * * (5 fields) is Unix cron syntax and will not work in Commerce Cloud.

sections.title: Abortable Jobs and the CronJob Lifecycle
sections.content:
Understanding the full lifecycle of a CronJob execution — and how to handle long-running tasks safely — is important for both the exam and production systems.

Making a Job Abortable

Override isAbortable() to return true, then check clearAbortRequestedIfNeeded(cronJob) periodically inside your perform() loop to respect abort requests from the HAC:

@Override
public PerformResult perform(CronJobModel cronJob) {
    List<OrderModel> orders = getOrdersToProcess();

    for (OrderModel order : orders) {

        // Check if an abort has been requested
        if (clearAbortRequestedIfNeeded(cronJob)) {
            return new PerformResult(
                CronJobResult.UNKNOWN,
                CronJobStatus.ABORTED);
        }

        processOrder(order);
    }

    return new PerformResult(
        CronJobResult.SUCCESS,
        CronJobStatus.FINISHED);
}

CronJob Logging

Commerce Cloud persists CronJob logs in the database. Each CronJob run creates a log entry accessible from the HAC under Background Processes → CronJobs. You can write to this log from within your job:

// Within perform() - logs are persisted to the DB
LOG.info("Processing order: " + order.getCode());

Node Groups and Cluster Execution

In a clustered environment, CronJobs can be restricted to run on specific nodes using node groups. The nodeGroup attribute on a CronJob specifies which cluster nodes are eligible to execute it. This prevents the same CronJob from running simultaneously on multiple nodes.

SingletonJobPerformable: For CronJobs that must never run concurrently (even across restarts), use the singletonJobPerformable pattern. It uses a database lock to ensure only one instance runs at a time, regardless of cluster configuration.

sections.title: Managing CronJobs in HAC and Best Practices
sections.content:
The HAC provides a full management interface for CronJobs under Background Processes → CronJobs. Understanding what you can do from HAC is tested in the exam.

HAC CronJob Management

  • View all CronJobs and their last execution result (SUCCESS, FAILURE, ERROR)
  • Trigger a CronJob manually — runs it immediately regardless of its trigger schedule
  • Abort a running CronJob — sends an abort signal (only effective if the job checks for it)
  • View logs — see the persisted log output of each run
  • Enable/disable CronJobs and Triggers

Best Practices

  • Always set user context — use admin or a dedicated service user at the start of perform()
  • Make long-running jobs abortable — check abort signals in loops
  • Use session language — set a language if the job uses localized data via FlexibleSearch
  • Avoid long transactions — commit in batches to avoid transaction timeouts and memory issues
  • Handle exceptions explicitly — return CronJobResult.ERROR rather than letting uncaught exceptions propagate; uncaught exceptions leave the CronJob in RUNNING status
// Pattern for batched processing
@Override
public PerformResult perform(CronJobModel cronJob) {
    userService.setCurrentUser(userService.getAdminUser());
    sessionService.setAttribute(
        CommonI18NService.LANGUAGE_SESSION_ATTR_KEY,
        commonI18NService.getLanguage("en"));

    int offset = 0;
    final int batchSize = 100;
    List<ProductModel> batch;

    do {
        if (clearAbortRequestedIfNeeded(cronJob)) {
            return new PerformResult(
                CronJobResult.UNKNOWN, CronJobStatus.ABORTED);
        }
        batch = productDao.findProductsBatch(offset, batchSize);
        processBatch(batch);
        modelService.saveAll(batch);
        modelService.clearTransactionalCache();
        offset += batchSize;
    } while (batch.size() == batchSize);

    return new PerformResult(
        CronJobResult.SUCCESS, CronJobStatus.FINISHED);
}
clearTransactionalCache(): In batch-processing CronJobs, call modelService.clearTransactionalCache() after each batch to release memory. Without this, every loaded Model accumulates in the session cache and large jobs will eventually run out of heap space.

questions.text: In the CronJob framework, what is the correct relationship between a Job, a CronJob, and a Trigger?
options: Job contains the schedule; CronJob contains the logic; Trigger connects them
Job contains the business logic; CronJob is the persisted run configuration referencing the Job; Trigger defines when to run the CronJob
CronJob contains the business logic; Job is the persisted item; Trigger is the Spring bean
Job and CronJob are interchangeable terms; Trigger defines both the logic and the schedule
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The three-part structure is: Job (Spring bean with logic) → CronJob (persisted config that references the Job) → Trigger (persisted schedule). This separation allows one Job to be reused across many CronJob configurations with different parameters and schedules.

questions.text: The 'springId' attribute on a ServicelayerJob item must exactly match the Spring bean ID of the job implementation.
questions.explanation: True. The platform uses the springId to look up the job's Spring bean at runtime. A mismatch between the database value and the actual bean ID causes a bean-not-found error when the CronJob is triggered. This is one of the most common CronJob configuration mistakes.

questions.text: A CronJob fails with an AccessDeniedException when calling a ProductService method. What is the most likely cause?
options: The CronJob's Spring bean is missing the @Transactional annotation
The CronJob runs as the anonymous user by default, which lacks the required permissions
The ServicelayerJob item has the wrong springId value
The Trigger's cron expression is invalid, causing the job to run in degraded mode
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. CronJobs execute under the anonymous user context by default. Most service calls require an authenticated user with appropriate permissions. The fix is to call userService.setCurrentUser(userService.getAdminUser()) at the start of perform().

questions.text: What does the Quartz cron expression '0 0 2 * * ?' mean?
options: Every 2 minutes
Every day at 02:00:00
Every 2 hours
At second 0, minute 0, on day 2 of every month
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. In Quartz 6-field format: 0 (second) 0 (minute) 2 (hour) * (every day of month) * (every month) ? (no specific day of week) = every day at 02:00:00. The leading seconds field distinguishes Quartz from 5-field Unix cron.

questions.text: A CronJob can have multiple Triggers, allowing it to run on different schedules simultaneously.
questions.explanation: True. Multiple Triggers can reference the same CronJob. This is useful for scenarios like running a full operation weekly and a lighter operation nightly, both pointing to the same CronJob configuration. Each Trigger independently fires the CronJob when its schedule matches.

questions.text: How does a Job implementation signal to the framework that it supports manual abort from the HAC?
options: By annotating the perform() method with @Abortable
By overriding isAbortable() to return true and periodically checking clearAbortRequestedIfNeeded()
By setting the 'abortable' property on the ServicelayerJob item in ImpEx
All jobs support abort automatically — no implementation change is needed
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Two things are required: override isAbortable() to return true (signals HAC to show the Abort button), and call clearAbortRequestedIfNeeded(cronJob) periodically inside the perform() loop (checks the database flag and returns true if an abort was requested). Without the periodic check, the abort signal is never acted on.

questions.text: What should be returned from perform() if a CronJob is successfully aborted mid-execution?
options: new PerformResult(CronJobResult.SUCCESS, CronJobStatus.FINISHED)
new PerformResult(CronJobResult.FAILURE, CronJobStatus.FINISHED)
new PerformResult(CronJobResult.UNKNOWN, CronJobStatus.ABORTED)
null — returning null signals an abort to the framework
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. When a job detects an abort signal and exits early, the correct return is CronJobResult.UNKNOWN (since the work is incomplete) with CronJobStatus.ABORTED. Returning SUCCESS when work is incomplete is misleading. Never return null — this causes a NullPointerException in the framework.

questions.text: Quartz cron expressions in Commerce Cloud use 6 fields, with the first field representing seconds.
questions.explanation: True. Commerce Cloud uses Quartz cron syntax which has 6 fields: second, minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week. This differs from Unix cron's 5 fields (which starts at minutes). A common mistake is writing 5-field expressions which are not valid in Commerce Cloud.

questions.text: Why should 'modelService.clearTransactionalCache()' be called periodically in a batch-processing CronJob?
options: It commits the current transaction to the database, ensuring data is persisted
It releases accumulated Models from the session cache, preventing OutOfMemoryErrors during large batch operations
It refreshes all loaded Models from the database to pick up changes made by other processes
It is required before calling modelService.saveAll() to avoid constraint violations
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Every Model loaded via ModelService is kept in the session cache for the duration of the session. In a CronJob processing thousands of items, this cache grows without bound. Calling clearTransactionalCache() after each batch releases those cached instances, keeping heap usage stable throughout the job.

questions.text: What happens if an uncaught exception propagates out of a CronJob's perform() method?
options: The CronJob is automatically retried according to the Trigger's retry policy
The CronJob status is set to FINISHED with result ERROR
The CronJob may be left in RUNNING status, making it appear still active in HAC
The platform catches all exceptions and always returns SUCCESS to prevent alerting
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. An uncaught exception can leave the CronJob in RUNNING status, which prevents it from being triggered again (the platform thinks it is still executing). Always wrap your perform() in a try/catch and explicitly return CronJobResult.ERROR with CronJobStatus.FINISHED on failure.

questions.text: In a clustered Commerce Cloud environment, a CronJob without a nodeGroup assignment will run simultaneously on all cluster nodes when its Trigger fires.
questions.explanation: False. The CronJob framework includes cluster awareness — without a nodeGroup, the platform uses a locking mechanism to ensure a CronJob runs on only one node at a time. However, configuring nodeGroup gives explicit control over which nodes are eligible, which is the recommended approach for production clusters.

questions.text: Where in the HAC can you manually trigger a CronJob to run immediately, regardless of its scheduled Trigger?
options: Console → Scripting Languages
Background Processes → CronJobs
Monitoring → Performance
Platform → Configuration
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The HAC's Background Processes → CronJobs section lists all CronJobs and provides controls to trigger them manually, abort running jobs, view logs, and enable or disable them. This is the primary operational interface for CronJob management.
